segments.s699-g97: وزانجای (compound 'وز' + 'انجای') = 'from that place'; it sets the point of departure. سوی is a preposition meaning 'toward' and governs the following noun. عماری here means 'a dwelling/chamber' (object of سوی). کشید is a past tense verb 'he drew/moved' (poetic usage 'کشیدن' = to move/advance) linking back to وزانجای and سوی to express motion from that place toward the chamber.
segments.s699-g98: بپرده is a circumstantial phrase 'behind a curtain' (ب + پرده). درون 'within/inside' further localizes the action. روی here is used as 'face' (not the prepositional sense) and is the direct object seen. مریم is the proper name 'Mary/Mariam'. بدید is the past tense 'he saw' and completes the clause, with the structure: (he) [behind a curtain inside] saw [the face of Mary].
segments.s699-g99: بپرسید is a past-tense verb 'he asked' (object implied). و simply links the two actions. بر دست او is a prepositional phrase 'upon her hand' with بر + object + possessive او. بوس داد is a common collocation meaning 'gave a kiss' (بوس as noun 'a kiss' and داد 'gave'). The clause describes two brief actions in sequence.
segments.s699-g100: ز here functions as a causal/ablative preposition 'because of' (poetic). دیدار 'the seeing/sight' is the noun governing the cause. آن خوب رخ is a noun phrase 'that fair/beautiful face'. گشت is the past-tense inchoative 'became' and شاد is the predicate adjective 'joyful'. Together: 'Because of the sight of that fair face, he became joyful.'
segments.s699-g101: بیاورد is a past-tense/ narrative verb 'he brought'. لشکر is the direct object 'the host/army'. به introduces the destination and پرده سرای is a compound 'curtain-chamber' or 'tented/curtained hall' (literally 'curtain' + 'sarā' = a place). The clause states that he led his host into that curtained space.
segments.s699-g102: نهفته is an adjective/participle 'hidden' modifying the whole following action. یکی 'a certain/one' qualifies ماه which here is used figuratively as 'a moon' = a beautiful person. را is the definite object marker marking ماه as the object of ساخت. ساخت is 'made/constructed' (past), and جای 'place' completes the verb: 'hidden, he made a place for a certain moon (i.e. a beauty)'.
segments.s699-g103: سخن گفت is a verbal phrase 'he spoke' or 'he conversed' (سخن = speech, گفت = spoke). و links the next action. بنشست 'he sat down' is a simple past verb; بااوسه ('with a kiss') is an ablative/circumstantial phrase describing how he sat. روز here is a temporal noun 'day' that connects to the time adverbial in the next segment.
segments.s699-g104: چهارم 'the fourth' is a temporal ordinal modifying the implied day. چو is a conjunction 'when'. بفروخت is a past verb 'sold/parted with' or 'was parted from' in narrative; here it introduces an event that occurred on that day. گیتی 'the world' + فروز 'bright/illuminating' form a compound-like epithet 'the world-bright one' (or an epithet/name); syntactically the phrase is the subject of بفروخت. The clause thus reads 'on the fourth, when the world-bright one parted/sold...' (ellipsis continues in following lines).
segments.s699-g113: This clause begins with the relative/connector که ("that/who") and gives the location of the action: زیر درفشش ("beneath his standard/banner"). برفتی is a poetic past verb meaning "went forth/marched"; هزار ("a thousand") is the numeral that logically follows the verb (word order in poetry is flexible). Together: "that beneath his banner went forth a thousand..."
segments.s699-g114: گزیده سواران is a noun phrase "chosen/elite horsemen." خنجر گزار is an adjectival/agentive phrase: خنجر ("dagger") + گزار (here functioning as "bearer/wielder"), so "dagger‑bearing"; in energetic, compact epic style the modifier follows the noun.
segments.s699-g115: چو here is the temporal conjunction "when." خسرو is the proper name (or title) "Khosrow/king." بدید is the simple past "saw." آن گزیده سپاه is the direct object "that chosen army/host"; adjective precedes or follows in variable order in epic Persian.
segments.s699-g116: This is a string of epithets attached to the horsemen: سواران ("horsemen/riders"); گردنکش here is an adjective meaning "rebellious/headstrong"; ورزمخواه is another adjective meaning "warlike/eager for battle." Persian epic often stacks descriptive adjectives after the noun.
segments.s699-g117: همی‌خواند is an older/poetic progressive/past form (همی‑ + present stem) indicating a continued action in narration: "kept singing/reciting." بر کردگار آفرین is a prepositional phrase meaning "praise to/upon the Creator" (word order inverted for poetic effect).
segments.s699-g118: که introduces a relative clause "who/that." چرخ آفرید is the verb phrase "created the sky" (چرخ = sky/heaven here); و زمان و زمین continues the list "and time and earth." آفرید is simple past "created."
segments.s699-g119: همان refers back to the same praise/song just mentioned. بر نیاطوس وبر لشکرش means "upon Niyātos and upon his army/host"; وبر is a poetic concatenation of و + بر ("and upon"), and لشکرش is لشکر + -ش ("his army").
segments.s699-g120: چه here is an exclamatory/how‑word meaning roughly "how/what (a)" introducing admiration. برنامور is an epithet meaning "renowned/famous." قیصر وکشورش names the imperial figure and his realm: قیصر (Caesar/emperor) and وکشورش ("and his country/realm"), with و‑ prefixed directly to the noun in one word (typical in older/poetic practice).
segments.s699-g25: ازان پس is a temporal adverbial: ازان (thereupon/after that) + پس (after/then) = "after that"; پرستنده is a deverbal noun meaning "admirer/worshipper" and here functions as a noun modifier for the following nominal phrase ماه روی (a compound meaning "moon‑faced" or "beautiful‑faced"). Word order is typical Persian: temporal phrase first, then the nominal head phrase.
segments.s699-g26: برفتند is the simple past, 3rd person plural (they went/they departed); it follows the noun phrase it governs. زایوان here functions as a locative/goal (they went up to the iwan/portico) and با + رنگ و بوی is an instrumental/attendant phrase meaning "with color and scent" (i.e. bearing beauty and perfume). The conjunctional v‑ in وبوی links بوی to رنگ.
segments.s699-g27: This is a nominal group: two adjectives خردمند و بیدار (wise and alert) precede the head noun پانصد غلام ("five hundred servants/slaves"). In Persian poetry adjectives can come before the numeral + noun; پانصد quantifies غلام and the whole phrase is the subject of the verb that follows.
segments.s699-g28: بیامد is past tense, 3rd singular/collective "came/arrived" used with a plural sense here; what follows (بزرین وسیمین ستام) is a descriptive string: بزرین and وسیمین are adjectival/epithetic descriptors ("great" and "silvery/gleaming"), and ستام is a noun/participle related to praise/renown. Together they form an appositive or predicate description of those who came.
segments.s699-g29: ز رومی is a prepositional phrase (ز = "from" + رومی = "Rûm/Byzantine") marking origin; همان نیز is a demonstrative + emphatic adverb ("that/that one likewise/also"); خادم is the noun "attendant/servant" and the numeral چهل follows to quantify the object associated with that attendant (poetic word order places the numeral after the noun here).
segments.s699-g30: پری چهره is a compound adjective/noun: پری (a fairy/beautiful woman) + چهره (face) = "fairy‑faced/beautiful‑faced." The following و شهره و دلگسل are coordinating adjectives ("and renowned and heart‑rending") that further describe the forty (i.e. the women associated with that attendant).
segments.s699-g31: وزان is a fused conjunction + prepositional item (و + ازان) meaning "and from that/and from them"; فیلسوفان رومی is the noun phrase "Roman philosophers/men of Rûm" that supplies the source or group. The following numeral چهار will quantify the next noun (elliptically understood as "men" or "ones").
segments.s699-g32: This cluster lists the attributes of the four: خردمند (wise) is an adjective, و با دانش is a coordinated descriptor where با + دانش ("with knowledge") yields "learned," and و نامدار ("and renowned") is a final epithet. Together they modify the implicit noun "men/men of learning."
segments.s699-g105: گزیده is a prepositive adjective meaning “selected / chosen,” modifying the following noun. سرایی here names a composed song/selection or performance (an abstract noun formed from the root سرای-), and بیاراستند is a 3rd-person plural past tense verb (بیاراست + -ند) meaning “they arranged/adorned/prepared.” Together the phrase means “they prepared a selected performance/entertainment.”
segments.s699-g106: نیاطوس را is a noun + the object marker را marking Niyatous as the direct object. پیش is an adverbial/prepositional element meaning “before / in the presence of,” placed immediately before the verb. اوخواستند is a 3rd-person plural past verb meaning “they desired / they asked for him / they summoned him” — so the clause literally reads “they requested Niyatous before him,” i.e. “they brought/summoned Niyatous forward.”
segments.s699-g107: This is a descriptive phrase listing garments/appearance. ابا likely names a cloak/outer garment (here treated as a clothing item), followed by سرگس “dishevelled” describing hair or appearance; و links the next item, کوت جنگی “short war-cloak” (کوت modified by جنگی ‘warlike/military’). بهم is an adverb meaning “together / in disorder.” The sequence is a coordinated noun/adjective list describing how they were dressed/appeared.
segments.s699-g108: سران سپه را is a noun phrase “the leaders of the army” with را marking them as the definite object/topic of the surrounding lines. همه “all” quantifies them, and بیش و کم is an idiomatic pair meaning “more and less” (i.e. “great and small,” “more or less”), so the whole segment says that the army’s chiefs, all of them, whether high or low, are being referred to.
segments.s699-g109: بدیشان (“to them”) is a prepositional-like clitic meaning “to/for them”; چنین is a discourse word “thus / so”; گفت is the past-tense verb “said.” کاکنون (کـا + کنون) is an emphatic temporal phrase meaning “now / at this moment.” سران is the noun “chiefs / leaders” and connects to the following interrogative. (Note: گفت here functions as the main reporting verb “he said.”)
segments.s699-g110: کدامند is an interrogative verb-form meaning “which are they?” (3rd-person plural). و is the coordinating ‘and’. مردان is the plural noun “men,” and جنگاوران is a plural noun/adjective “warriors / warlike men,” here apposed to مردان to ask which persons are the leaders and which are the warriors.
segments.s699-g111: نیاطوس is the subject (proper name). بگزید is an archaic/classical past tense 3rd-person singular of گزیندن (to choose) — the prefix ب- is a classical marker. هفتاد مرد is the direct object “seventy men.” The order is Subject–Verb–Object.
segments.s699-g112: که introduces a relative/purpose clause (“that/so that / who”). آورد is the verb “bring / carry,” and گیرند is a 3rd-person plural form meaning “they take / they seize” (here part of the verbal idea “to take/bring” for the coming action). روز نبرد is a temporal phrase “the day of battle.” The whole clause gives the purpose/role of those chosen: they are to be brought/taken on the day of battle.
segments.s699-g89: دل خسرو is a genitive/possessive phrase where the possessed noun دل (heart) comes before its possessor خسرو (Khosrow) — “Khosrow's heart.” The following prepositional phrase از لشکر نامدار gives the cause or object of that feeling: از here means “because of / on account of” and لشکر نامدار = “the famed army.”
segments.s699-g90: بخندید is simple past (3rd person sg.) meaning “laughed/smiled” with دل خسرو as its logical subject. چون گل is a simile (“like a flower”), and بوقت بهار here gives the time/season (“at the time of spring”); note the lemma for بوقت in the provided list does not include this temporal sense, so that sense is flagged below.
segments.s699-g91: This phrase strings modifiers with the head noun دل: روشن (bright) and راد (generous/noble) qualify دل. راتیز here functions as a predicative/adverbial idea ‘swift/quick’ and کرد is the past-tense causative/resultative ‘made/did’ — together conveying that his bright, noble heart caused him to become/act swiftly. These are poetic/archaic epithets and راتیز is an older adjective meaning ‘swift’ or ‘active’.
segments.s699-g92: مران is a proper name (the actor). باره را is a definite object marked by را (here باره = ‘burden/load’). پاشنه خیز is a compound: پاشنه ‘heel’ + خیز ‘a springing/advance’; together they describe a heel-stamp or a spring forward on the heel. کرد is the past verb ‘did/made’ — so the whole means that Maran caused the load to be set in motion by a heel-stamp (i.e. sprang forward).
segments.s699-g93: نیاطوس را دید: را marks نیاطوس as the definite object and دید is simple past ‘saw’. در برگرفت is a light-verb phrase where در (in/into) + برگرفت (took up / seized) together mean ‘took him up / embraced him / took him inside’ — a single verbal idea formed by preposition + verb common in classical Persian.
segments.s699-g94: بپرسیدن is an infinitival form ‘to ask’ (here part of an action he undertook), آزادی is the object ‘freedom/liberty/release’, and اندرگرفت is past-tense ‘took up / began (doing)’ — a frequent classical compound where اندرگرفت means ‘he began’ or ‘he set about’ (i.e. he began to ask for freedom).
segments.s699-g95: ز قیصر is a prepositional phrase ‘from/with regard to the Caesar’ (poetic usage). که introduces a relative clause ‘who…’. برداشت here is used in the sense ‘underwent / bore’ (classical usage ‘to take on/experience’), but that specific sense is not among the provided sense-choices, so it is flagged as unavailable below. زانگونه رنج = ‘such hardship/suffering.’
segments.s699-g96: ابا here is the verb ‘to refuse / be unwilling’ (classical use), taking رنج دیگر (‘further/other suffering’) as its object. تهی کرد is a compound of the adjective تهی ‘empty’ and the light verb کرد ‘made’, so تهی کرد گنج means ‘he emptied the treasure’ (he caused the treasure to become empty). The sequence conveys that, refusing further suffering, he emptied his treasury.
segments.s699-g57: This clause literally orders as Subject/Verb/Object/PP in a poetic register: همه (all/everything) is the object scope, یاد کرد is past tense 3rd‑singular ('made a record/remembered' — here 'recorded'), این is the demonstrative 'this', and به نامه درون is a prepositional phrase 'in the inner/book' (نامه = book/record, درون = inside). The verb کرد is the main past tense anchor linking the whole clause.
segments.s699-g58: برفتند is past plural ('they went/departed'); با introduces accompaniment or means ('with'), followed by دانَش 'knowledge' and رهنمون 'guidance' (here 'guidance' rather than a person). The clause is a simple past action describing the group's departure accompanied by knowledge/guidance.
segments.s699-g59: ستاره شمر is a noun phrase where ستاره 'star' is qualified by the name شمر ('Shamr'), i.e. 'Shamr's star' or 'the star of Shamr.' پیش here is adverbial ('forward/in front') and با رهنمای ('with a guide') states accompaniment; the phrase describes the star/person moving forward accompanied by a guide.
segments.s699-g60: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/which' or purpose/explanatory clause). تارفتنش is تـا + رفتنش, a nominalized phrase meaning 'until his leaving' or more idiomatically 'his departure'; کی is the interrogative 'when'; به آید is present/future third‑person 'will come/occur' (آید = 'come/happen'), and ز جای means 'from the place' (ز = from, جای = place). The whole clause asks/indicates when his leaving from his place will take place.
segments.s699-g61: The verb جنبید is past tense 3rd‑singular ('he moved/stirred'). In poetic order the subject قیصر ('Caesar') follows the verb, and به بهرام روز is a temporal phrase 'on (the) Bahram day' (به + proper name + روز = 'on the day of Bahram'). This presents a past event tied to a named auspicious day.
segments.s699-g62: This is an instrumental/causal phrase: به + نیک اختر و فال gives the means/omen ('by/with a good/auspicious star and an omen'), گیتی فروز is a compound predicate where گیتی 'the world' is qualified by فروز ('illuminating/bright' or 'that makes bright'). The clause says the world was brightened by an auspicious star and omen.
segments.s699-g63: دو منزل is a quantified time/distance phrase 'two stages' (منزل = stage of a journey). همی‌رفت is the past continuous/imperfective form (همی‑ prefix marking ongoing action) 'was going/traveling', قیصر is the subject, and به راه indicates direction or 'along the road.' The clause describes Caesar's continuing progress.
segments.s699-g64: سدیگر functions as an indefinite/alternative subject 'another/the other (one)' (literally 'another obstacle/one'), بیامد is past tense 'came', and به پیش سپاه is a locative phrase 'before/in front of the army' (به پیش = in the presence of / before, سپاه = army). The clause states that another came forward before the army.
segments.s699-g49: یکی here means 'a certain (man)' introducing an individual. راز دارست is an archaic/poetic compression of داراست ('has, possesses'), so the verb phrase means 'had/possessed a secret'. بالوی نیز (بالوی + نیز) functions adverbially: بالوی = 'on top of that / besides' and نیز = 'also', so together they emphasize that this is an additional fact about him. Note the subject phrase precedes the predicate because of the poetic order.
segments.s699-g50: که introduces a subordinate content clause ('that…'). نفروشد is the negative form of the verb 'فروخت/فروختن' here meaning 'would not sell / does not sell' (classical present/subjunctive form with negative prefix ن-). آزادگان را is the definite object — آزادگان 'the free (men)' with را marking them as object. بچیز = ب + چیز = 'for/with anything' → 'for any thing' (i.e. 'for any price'); the whole clause says 'that he would not sell the free men for anything.'
segments.s699-g51: چوخراد برزین here is a name (two-name sequence). نبیند is the negative present/subjunctive form of دیدن ('see') used narratively, so with the negative it yields 'saw not / no one saw'. Placement of نبیند before کسی gives the negative scope: کسی ('anyone') in this negative context functions as 'no one' — 'no one saw Chokhrad Borzin.'
segments.s699-g52: اگر is the conditional 'if'. چند here functions adverbially as 'a little / for some time'. ماند is the verb 'remain/stay' (3rd person), so 'if he remained a while'. بگیتی is a locative/adverbial phrase 'in the world' (ب- + گیتی). بسی is the predicate particle meaning 'it is enough / that suffices', so the clause reads 'if he remained some time in the world, that would be enough.'
segments.s699-g53: بران is a causal/relating adverb meaning 'for that / thereupon' (linking back to what was just said). آفریدش is the verb 'created' with the enclitic object -ش = 'him' ('created him'). خدای جهان ('God of the world') is the agent; in poetic order the verb appears before the subject and object enclitic, so verb+object then subject.
segments.s699-g54: که + تا together introduce a purpose/result clause: 'so that / in order that'. آشکارا is an adverb 'openly / manifestly'. شود is the subjunctive/passive-like form of شدن 'become / be made', giving 'may be made / become manifest'. ز + و here function together as the contraction 'زو' = 'from him/of him' (z- = from, and the second element represents the pronoun), and نهان means 'hidden'. Altogether: 'so that what was hidden in/from him should be revealed.'
segments.s699-g55: چو is a simile particle 'as / like'. خورشید تابنده is the noun phrase 'the shining sun' (noun + participial adjective). او is the pronoun 'he' (the person being compared). بی‌بدیست is a classical/perhaps compressed predicate meaning 'is without equal / peerless' (بی‌بدی + است in poetic spelling); it predicates the simile ('he is peerless like the shining sun').
segments.s699-g56: همه is the quantifier 'all'. کار و کردار is a paired noun phrase 'works and deeds' (parallel nouns joined by و). او is the genitive/pronominal 'his'. ایزدیست is the predicate 'is divine / godly' (ایزدی + است); the whole NP 'all his works and deeds' is predicated as godly.
segments.s699-g17: ز introduces a partitive/source phrase ('from, out of') and governs the following coordinated nouns. گستردنیها is a poetic plural noun here (lit. 'those that are spread') functioning as 'hangings' or 'draperies'; و links it to دیبای روم, where دیبا (dībā) is a textile name 'brocade/silk' and روم specifies origin ('of Rome/Byzantium'). Word order is the typical Persian ezāfe-less listing common in older poetry.
segments.s699-g18: به here introduces manner/material and is used with a following noun phrase ('with/in gold‑figure'); زر modifies پیکر (noun 'figure, form'), so به زر پیکر = 'with/of gold figure(s)'. The conjunction و connects that phrase to the next source phrase introduced by از ('and from/of'). بریشمش is 'its silk' (بریشم + possessive -ش), and بوم ('canvas/cloth') names the resulting fabric. The clause therefore lists two features: gold-figured decoration and silk cloth.
segments.s699-g19: همان is a demonstrative 'the same/that very', introducing the next items in the catalogue. یاره is an older/poetic word; in a literal sense it means 'companion/beloved' but in this enumerative, material context it functions as a named object (an item of ornament or regalia). و simply coordinates that with طوق ('necklace/collar'), and با + گوشوار ('with earrings') specifies an accompanying set. The whole segment is a noun list introduced by همان.
segments.s699-g20: This is a compact noun phrase: a numeral (سه) + head noun (تاج 'crown'), followed by two adjectives in postposition: گرانمایه ('precious') and گوهرنگار ('gem‑set' or 'jeweled'). Persian permits stacked modifiers after the noun in poetic diction; together they mean 'three precious, gem-encrusted crowns.'
segments.s699-g21: عماری is a noun 'a building/edifice' (architectural), followed by بیاراست which is a past/participial verb 'adorned / he adorned' (in poetic narrative the subject is omitted). زرین is an adjective 'golden' placed after the verb, here qualifying the edifice as 'golden' or 'gold‑adorned'; چهار ('four') follows as a numeral modifier — together giving 'a four (part) golden edifice' or 'a golden edifice of four (units)'.
segments.s699-g22: جلیلش is جلیل ('majestic, venerable') plus the possessive/agentive suffix -ش ('its'/'his'), so 'its majestic (part)'. پر here is an adjectival predicate 'full (of)', and ازگوهر ('of gems') gives the content. شاهوا ر is split in the line: شاهوا is an archaic/poetic qualifier meaning 'kingly' or 'of the king', and ر is the enclitic object marker (ِـر/را) attached in older orthography; the whole phrase reads 'full of the kingly gems' (i.e. the royal jewels).
segments.s699-g23: This segment begins with the numeral phrase چهل مهد ('forty cradles' or 'forty places of rest'). دیگر ('other/another') qualifies the forty as additional items. بد is an archaic/linking word here in poetic usage that connects the noun to the material specification that follows (its precise lexical senses are uncertain in modern Persian), and از آبنوس means 'of/from ebony' (آبنوس = ebony). Grammatically, the sequence names forty additional cradles, specified as being of ebony.
segments.s699-g24: ز introduces the material/quality clause 'from/of gems' (ز گوهر). درفشان is a participial adjective 'shining, glittering' modifying the implied subject (the cradles). چو is the simile marker 'like/as', and چشم خروس is a simile image 'the eye of a rooster' — a frequent poetic comparison for a bright, gleaming spot. The whole phrase describes the jewel-studded gleam 'shining like a rooster's eye.'
segments.s699-g73: The clause has a nominative name followed by an adjectival qualifier and a possessed noun plus the past copula. نیاطوس is the subject; جنگی is an adjective meaning 'warlike' that qualifies the following noun برادرش ('his brother', where -ش is the 3rd-person possessive). بود is the past copula 'was' closing the clause.
segments.s699-g74: بدان here functions as a fused prepositional/demonstrative phrase 'in/at that' (بدان جنگ = 'in that battle'). جنگ is the noun 'battle/war'. سالار لشکرش is a nominal predicate 'commander of his army' (لشکر with the possessive -ش), and بود is the past copula 'was' closing the clause; the subject (he/Niyātūs) is implied.
segments.s699-g75: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him/unto him'. گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' with the speaker following it (مریم). به خون خویش is a prepositional phrase 'by/with (her) own blood' where خویش is a reflexive/possessive referring to the speaker. تست here is an archaic/classical copula meaning 'thou art' (not the modern noun 'test'); this sense is not listed in the provided sense options.
segments.s699-g76: بران 'upon that' (a demonstrative/adverbial particle) + برنهادم is a first-person verb form built on نهاد with the prefix بر and final -م ('I placed / I will place' in poetic register; here it has a volitive/performative sense 'I will stake/place upon that'). که introduces the subordinate clause 'that', and هم‌کیش تست is the clause 'you are a fellow-believer' where هم‌کیش = 'coreligionist/fellow-believer' and تست is again the archaic copula 'thou art' (sense not in the provided list).
segments.s699-g77: سپردم is the first-person past tense 'I entrusted/handed over'. The following pronoun تو (with را) marks the person to whom something is entrusted; را here serves as the object/topic marker in this poetic order. دختر is the object 'the daughter/maiden'. وخواسته is a compacted phrase meaning 'and (as) requested/desired'. The clause thus means 'I have entrusted the maiden to you as asked.'
segments.s699-g78: سپاهی is 'the army/warriors'. برین گونه is a fixed adverbial phrase meaning 'in this manner' (بر + این/ین + گونه = 'upon/at-this manner'), and آراسته is the past participle/adjective 'adorned/arrayed'. Together: 'the army is thus arrayed' (predicate adjective phrase describing the army).
segments.s699-g79: نیاطوس is the subject. یکسر is an adverb meaning 'entirely/at once'. پذیرفت is the past tense 'accepted'. از وی is a prepositional phrase 'from him/her/it' (از + وی). The clause reads 'Niyātūs accepted outright (from him/her/it)', with the agent/object reference given by از وی.
segments.s699-g80: بگفتند is a conventional narrative phrase 'they said' or 'it was said'. و is the conjunction 'and'. گریان is a participial/adjectival form 'weeping/crying'. بپیچید is a past-tense verb 'turned' (پیچیدن with prefix ب-), and روی here means 'face' — together 'and, weeping, he turned his face (away)'.
segments.s699-g121: بدان مهتران گفت is a narrative clause: بدان (classical prepositional phrase, literally 'to those/thereupon') + مهتران (plural of مهتر, 'chiefs, elders') + گفت (past of گفتن, 'said'). اگر begins a conditional clause ('if') and کردگار is the subject of that condition ('the Creator/God'). Word order follows classical Persian: reporting verb + conditional particle + subject of the conditional.
segments.s699-g122: مرا یارباشد گه کارزار should be read as مرا (the object pronoun 'me', here written as م + را), یار باشد ('be friend/ally' — باشد is subjunctive/potential meaning 'be' or 'may be') and گه کارزار ('when the time/occasion of battle' — گه = 'occasion, time', کارزار = 'battle'). The literal order is 'God be ally to me when (the) battle (time)'; باشد expresses potential/contingency in the conditional.
segments.s699-g123: توانایی خویش پیداکنم = توانایی ('ability') + خویش (reflexive 'one's own', here 'my own' — split in the data as خوی + ش) + پیداکنم (پیدا + کنم, literally 'I find/make found'; present-subjunctive form used as first-person prospective 'I will find' or 'I shall discover'). The noun phrase (ability + possessive) precedes the verb, as in Persian.
segments.s699-g124: زمین را بکوکب ثریا کنم parses as زمین را (زمین + را, 'the earth' with direct-object marker) followed by a compound predicate meaning 'make (it) a star/raise it to the Pleiades': بکوکب (an epithetic/compound element referring to 'as/into a star') + ثریا کنم (ثریا = 'the Pleiades', کنم = 'I make' in present-subjunctive used prospectively). The object marker را clings to زمین and the verb کنم governs the whole action.
segments.s699-g125: نباشد جزاندیشه دوستان is elliptical/classical: نباشد is a negative subjunctive/optative ('let there not be' / 'there is not'), جز اندیشه = 'except the thought(s) of' and دوستان = 'friends'. Together it expresses ‘let there be nothing except the friends' thoughts’ or 'there is nothing but the thoughts of friends'; جز introduces the sole exception.
segments.s699-g126: فلک یارومهر ردان بوستان: فلک (here 'the heaven' or 'heavenly/fateful power') is the subject, یار و مهر ('friend and favor/kindness') is a nominal predicate or blessing, ردان is a present-participial/agentive form meaning 'driving away/expelling', and بوستان ('garden') is the object affected. The clause functions as a benediction: 'May heaven be friend and favor, driving away (harm) from the garden.' Word order places the subject first, predicate and participle after.
segments.s699-g41: مهان را is the definite object (مهان 'guests' + را object marker). همان before اسپ is a demonstrative meaning 'that same/the very' qualifying اسپ 'horse'. و connects اسپ and دینار. داد is the past-tense verb 'gave' with an understood subject (he/they). The clause follows Persian SOV order with the verb final.
segments.s699-g42: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing a limiting phrase; شایسته modifies هر چیز as 'appropriate/befitting', and بسیار is an adverb/adjective meaning 'in great quantity/abundantly'. داد is again the past 'gave'. Word order places the qualifying phrase before the verb in typical Persian poetic inversion.
segments.s699-g43: چنین گفت is a fixed phrase 'thus he said' (چنین 'thus/so' + گفت 'said'); the following phrase کای زیردستان شاه is a vocative address: کای 'O' + زیردستان شاه 'subjects of the king'. The verb 'said' takes the vocative as the addressee of the ensuing speech.
segments.s699-g44: سزد‌گر functions as a conditional/optative sense 'if it is fitting' (literally 'fitting' used to introduce what should be done). بر + آرید is 'place/bring upon' (بر 'on/upon' + آرید, here a jussive/imperative-like form 'bring/place'). گردن is the object 'neck' (i.e. 'onto the neck'), and بماه is a simile 'like the moon' (describing the appearance). Together they instruct to put something upon the neck so that it looks like the moon.
segments.s699-g45: ز + گستهم is a poetic genitive-like phrase 'from my wandering' (گستهم is a first-person perfect used nominally). شایسته‌تر is the comparative 'more worthy', and در جهان 'in the world' limits the scope. The phrase sets up the comparison/context for the negative clause that follows.
segments.s699-g46: نخیزد is a negative present/past-general verb meaning 'does not arise/appear' (here idiomatically 'is not found'); کسی 'anyone/someone' is the indefinite subject, از میان 'from among', and مهان 'the guests' is the class being considered. The clause therefore states that no one from among the guests appears/exists who is more worthy.
segments.s699-g47: چوشاپور is a proper name (here used as a personal name), مهتر is the title 'chief/headman', and کرانجی specifies his locality or affiliation 'of Karanj'. بود is the past copula 'was'. The noun phrase follows the typical nominal + title + origin pattern before the copula.
segments.s699-g48: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. اندر سخنها 'in the speeches/among words' gives the domain, and می‌انجی بود combines the imperfective marker می- with انجى (here a root related to assemblies/gatherings) plus بود as auxiliary, yielding 'he was one of the assemblies/was prominent in gatherings'—i.e., he was notable in discourse at assemblies. The construction expresses habitual/past characteristic.
segments.s699-g1: وزان پس چو دانست کامد سپاه is a temporal subordinate clause plus the event clause. وزان پس (literally 'from that, then') is a classical time phrase meaning 'after that / thereafter'. چو + دانست introduces a temporal 'when' clause ('when he knew'), and کامد (an archaic/poetic present/future form) indicates the coming/arrival of the army; سپاه is the direct noun 'army' and follows the verb in this poetic word order.
segments.s699-g2: جهان شد ز گرد سواران سیاه: جهان is the subject 'the world'; شد is the past/resultative 'became' linking the subject to the following prepositional phrase. ز گرد (= 'from/around') introduces the surrounding or pervasive cause, followed by the genitive phrase سواران سیاه 'black horsemen' (noun + postpositive adjective). The whole means the world was encircled/filled by black horsemen.
segments.s699-g3: گزین کرد زان رومیان صدهزار is a verbal phrase where گزین + کرد is the compound past 'chose/selected'. زان ('from those') marks the source (those Romans), and صدهزار is the numeral object 'one hundred thousand' referring to how many were chosen. Word order follows classical style with the verb before the quantified noun phrase.
segments.s699-g4: همه نامدار ازدرکارزار modifies the chosen men: همه ('all' / 'every one') + نامدار ('renowned') describes them collectively, and ازدرکارزار is a fused prepositional phrase 'from the field/battle' (classical idiom) indicating that their renown comes from fighting. The phrase attributes fame in battle to all the selected men.
segments.s699-g5: سلیح و درم خواست واسپان جنگ lists the items demanded. سلیح 'weapons' and درم 'dirham(s)' are coordinated by و. خواست is the past/requesting verb 'asked for/ordered'. واسپان جنگ functions as a compound object 'war-horses/steeds of war' (a poetic form for war-horses), completing the triple of supplies requested; the whole is an object-list governed by خواست.
segments.s699-g6: سرآمد برو روزگار درنگ is a compact narrative clause. سرآمد ('the foremost' / 'leader, best of them') is the agent; برو here is used in a narrative sense 'set out' / 'went forth' (a poetic immediate form), and روزگار درنگ ('time/no delay') is an elliptical comment: literally 'time [had] no delay' — i.e. they set out without delay. The phrase uses classical ellipsis (the copula or negative is omitted) to express prompt departure.
segments.s699-g7: یکی دخترش بود مریم بنام states existence: یکی ('a certain') + دخترش ('his daughter') with بود (past 'was') introduces her, and مریم بنام ('named Maryam') is a postposed name phrase ('called Maryam'). The order is typical: indefinite + noun + copula + name-ascribed.
segments.s699-g8: خردمند و با سنگ و با رای وکام lists attributes/possessions. خردمند is an adjective 'wise' describing the daughter. The coordinated prepositional phrases با سنگ ('with stones' = with jewels) and با رای و کام ('with counsel/plan and with will/desire') enumerate qualities or endowments; و links the items. The phrase uses classical postpositive adjectives and repeated با to emphasize each possession.
segments.s699-g9: بخسرو is the contracted poetic form of بهِ خسرو, a prepositional phrase 'to the king' that functions as indirect object; فرستاد is the simple past narrative 'he sent/ dispatched' and appears before the prepositional phrase (a common poetic order). به آیین دین is a manner/instrument phrase 'in the rite(s) of the faith' (به + noun) specifying the ceremony by which the sending takes place.
segments.s699-g10: همی‌خواست is a past-tense narrative form (همی- prefix + خواست) meaning 'he wished/desired'; ازکردگار is the source phrase 'from the Creator' (از + کردگار). آفرین here is a noun 'praise/blessing/acclamation' that is the object of 'he wished' (i.e., he wished a blessing from God).
segments.s699-g11: بپذرفت is a past-tense verb 'he accepted/received' with دخترش as its direct object 'his daughter'. گستهم گرد appears after the object as an attributive phrase (an epithet modifying 'daughter'); the two words form a fixed poetic epithet and so are placed postpositively to qualify the daughter.
segments.s699-g12: به آیین نیکو is a prepositional phrase 'in/according to a good/appropriate rite' (به + آیین, modified by نیکو). بخسرو is again 'to the king' and سپرد is the past verb 'he entrusted/handed over' — together the clause means he entrusted her to the king in that good ceremony (prepositional phrases precede the verb in this poetic order).
segments.s699-g13: وزان پس is a connective adverbial 'and from that / thereafter' (وز + آن + پس compressed), introducing a following action. بیاورد is a narrative/past form 'he brought' (here used to report the action) and چندان جهیز is the quantified object 'so much trousseau' (چندان 'so much' modifying جهیز 'trousseau/dowry goods').
segments.s699-g14: کزان is a relative/source phrase 'from which' that links back to the trousseau; کند شد is a two-part verbal phrase where کند (do/make) + شد (become) combine to yield a resultative/passive sense 'were made / came to be made'. بارگیهای تیز is the resultant noun phrase 'barag- items (poetic items) described as تیز' — in context this names the products fashioned from the trousseau. Grammatically, the clause says 'from which were made [those] …'.
segments.s699-g15: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'; زرینه is an adjective/nominal 'golden (things)'; و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'; گوهر شاهوار is a noun phrase 'regal/kingly jewels' where شاهوار post-modifies گوهر. The segment enumerates valuable materials/objects.
segments.s699-g16: ز یاقوت is again 'from rubies' (ز + یاقوت). وز here represents the poetic fusion of و + ز ('and from'), introducing جامه زرنگار 'brocaded/brocade garments' (جامه = garment, زرنگار = brocade). The segment continues the enumeration of luxurious items.
segments.s699-g65: فرمود is the main past verb meaning 'ordered/said' and here takes a subordinate clause introduced by تا (sense: 'so that/that'); تا مریم آمد به پیش is that subordinate clause meaning 'that Maryam should come forward/into his presence.' The initial به is the usual prepositional particle ('to/for') collocated with فرمود in older/poetic usage, and به پیش = 'into (his) presence / before (him)'.
segments.s699-g66: سخن گفت is a common compound meaning 'spoke' or 'held speech' (noun + verb); با او marks the addressee 'with him/her'. ز اندازه بیش is an adverbial phrase 'beyond measure' (ز = 'from/of' in poetic use, اندازه = 'degree/extent', بیش = 'more/excess'), modifying how she spoke.
segments.s699-g67: بدو گفت is 'he said to her/him' (بدو = 'to him/to her', here an indirect-object clitic or prepositional adverb). دامن ز ایرانیان is the object phrase: 'your skirt/hem from the Iranians' — elliptically meaning 'keep your skirt from the Iranians' (the poet omits an explicit verb or object marker that prose would supply).
segments.s699-g68: This is a pair of imperatives joined by و: نگه‌دار = 'keep/hold/preserve' (imperative), and مگشای = negative imperative 'do not open / do not undo.' بند ازمیان = 'the bond/tie from between (us)' — i.e. 'do not loosen the bond between us.' The order places the verb before its object (common in Persian imperatives).
segments.s699-g69: برهنه (adjective) + نباید (impersonal negative 'must not/should not') gives the prohibition 'you must not be exposed/uncovered.' که introduces the following clause explaining the reason: 'that the king sees you.' خسرو is used as 'the king/sovereign', and را marks تو as the definite object of ببیند.
segments.s699-g70: ببیند is the verb of the subordinate clause 'sees' (here used with the preceding نباید ... که to express 'must not be seen by...'). The second که introduces an explanatory clause 'that (so that) a thing/new event may happen to you.' کاری here stands for 'a thing / an event' and رسد is used in the sense 'to happen / befall,' modified by نو ('new'). تو را again is the object marked by را.
segments.s699-g71: بگفت این = 'he said this' (simple past). و connects to بدرود کردش 'he made/said farewell to her' (کردش = 'did it to her' = 'he bade her farewell'); به مهر 'with affection' specifies the manner. The verb کردش carries the object clitic ش = 'her'.
segments.s699-g72: This clause contains an optative wish: بادا = 'may (it be)' and یار تو = 'your companion'. برفتن is a verbal noun meaning 'departure' or 'to depart' and سپهر 'the heavens' (here the thing wished to be companion). Word order is poetic: 'May the heavens be your companion in departing.'
segments.s699-g33: بدیشان (بِ + ایشان) is a prepositional phrase 'to them' placed before the verb; بگفت is the past tense 'he told/said'. آنچ بایست گفت is a nominal/relative clause 'that which ought to be said' where بایست is a modal 'ought/must' and گفت is here a deverbal noun ('the saying' / 'what is said') serving as the object of بگفت.
segments.s699-g34: همان resumes the preceding content ('the same thing'); نیز 'also' adds that he did something else with it. با governs the comitative phrase 'with Mary' (مریم). اندرنهفت is a poetic past-form (اندر + نهفتن) meaning 'hid/kept hidden inside', so the whole segment = 'he also hid that same thing with Mary'.
segments.s699-g35: از introduces an ablative/partitive list 'from' or 'out of'; آرام and کام are nouns in a coordinated series ('calm' and 'desire'). وز is a poetic connective here functioning like و 'and' (poetic spelling/contraction); بایستگی closes the list as 'necessity/obligation'. The phrase enumerates sources or motives.
segments.s699-g36: همان points back to 'that same (thing)'; بخشش is 'a giving/gift' (here sense = grant/donation). The coordinated خورد و شایستگی list follows: خورد is used nominally (classical usage from خوردن) meaning a received portion / what is taken/appointed, and شایستگی is 'worthiness' or 'merit'. Together they enumerate the kinds of distribution or status given.
segments.s699-g37: پس 'then' opens the clause; از + خواسته together mean 'on account of the request' / 'at the asking'. کرد is the past tense verb 'made/did'; رومی is the subject 'the Roman' and شمار is the direct noun 'number/count' (poetic word order places the verb before subject/object for emphasis).
segments.s699-g38: فزون 'more' or 'in excess' heads the comparative predicate. بد here is an archaic/poetic form of بودن (was) rather than the adjective 'bad' — it functions as the past copula ('was more than'). ز is the poetic comparative 'than/from', and the following numerals give the amount ('three hundred thousand thousands', i.e. an emphatic very large number).
segments.s699-g39: فرستاد is the past verb 'he sent'. هر کس که is the indefinite relative 'everyone who / each person who'. بد بردرش here is a compact/poetic locative: بردرش = بر درِش 'at his door / before him', and the preceding بد is an archaic/poetic rendering of به 'to' (thus 'to those at his threshold'). Together the phrase means 'he sent to each person who was before him'.
segments.s699-g40: ز 'of/from' introduces the material or composition; گوهر نگار افسری is a noun phrase 'a diadem adorned with gems' — گوهر 'gems', نگار here functions adjectivally ('adorned/ornate with') and افسری is 'diadem/tiara'. بر سرش is the locative 'on his head' (بر + سر + ـش his).
segments.s699-g81: همی‌رفت is a literary/epic past-continuous form meaning 'was going' (iterative/habitual past). لشکر is the subject 'the army'. به راه is a prepositional phrase 'on the road / on the way' (به as direction/onto). وریغ is unclear or irregular in this edition; it does not fit the supplied senses and appears here as an interposed item before the name that follows (treated as non-standard or editorial), so it is left as an unresolved element linking to the next phrase.
segments.s699-g82: نیا طوس together refer to the personal name Niyātus/Tūs (here functioning as the leader). در پیش is a locative phrase 'in front / before (them)', with در 'in' and پیش 'before, ahead'. با + [گرز و تیغ] is the instrumental 'with mace and sword' (با = 'with', گرز = 'mace', وتیغ = 'and sword').
segments.s699-g83: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. بشنید is the simple past 'he heard'. خسروکه represents 'Khosrow' together with the clause-linker که: the sequence means 'when Khosrow heard (that)…' with the reported-clause following (that the army had come). آمد سپاه is the content of the report 'the host came/arrived'.
segments.s699-g84: ازان = 'from that / out of that' (locative source). شارستان = 'town/city'. برد here is the past of بردن 'to take/lead' (the supplied dictionary senses did not list the verbal sense, so it is noted as the transitive verb 'led/took'). لشکر is the object 'the army' and به راه is the directional complement 'onto the road' (به = toward/into). Word order places the object before the directional phrase, typical in Persian narrative.
segments.s699-g85: چو آمد is 'when (they) came'. پدیدار here functions as an adjectival/predicative 'visible/appeared'. گرد + سران means 'around the chiefs' or 'about the leaders' (گرد = 'around', سران = 'leaders, chiefs'). The phrase is a poetic, somewhat inverted order meaning 'when the leaders appeared'.
segments.s699-g86: This is a nominal series describing who came into view: درفش 'banner/standard' (here 'banner-bearers' by apposition), سواران 'horsemen/riders', جوشن 'cuirass/armor' (i.e. those wearing armor). وران is a bound/poetic element forming agentive compounds (joshon-varān = 'those clad in cuirass'); the supplied senses for وران do not match this grammatical usage and are therefore treated as non-applicable in the dictionary list.
segments.s699-g87: همی‌رفت is the habitual/continuous past 'was going'. لشکر is the subject 'the army'. بکردار is an attributive adjective 'of good conduct / well‑ordered / virtuous' describing the army's disposition or arrangement, and گرد indicates arrangement 'around' (here: organized around another element). The phrase therefore says the army moved 'in good order' around something (the following phrase names that something).
segments.s699-g88: A coordinated descriptive phrase: سواران بیدار = 'horsemen who are alert' (بیدار here 'alert/awake'), و = 'and', مردان مرد is a fixed emphatic epithet meaning 'men of men' → 'truly heroic men' (مردان = 'men/heroes', مرد intensifies to 'hero/valiant man'). This lists the army's core as alert riders and heroic men.

sentences.s699-s49: From that place he moved toward a chamber, and behind a curtain within he saw Mary’s face.
sentences.s699-s50: He asked (her), and kissed her hand; at the sight of that fair face he grew joyful.
sentences.s699-s51: He brought the host into the curtained chamber; hidden away, he made a place for a certain moon-like one.
sentences.s699-s52: He spoke and sat down with a kiss; on the fourth day, when the world-bright one parted, ...
sentences.s699-s57: That, beneath his standard, marched forth a thousand chosen horsemen, dagger‑bearers.
sentences.s699-s58: When Khosrow saw that chosen host, the horsemen — headstrong and eager for battle,
sentences.s699-s59: they were loudly singing praise to the Creator, who created the sky and time and the earth.
sentences.s699-s60: the same (praise) upon Niyātos and upon his host — and likewise upon the famed Caesar and his realm.
sentences.s699-s13: After that, the admirers of the moon‑faced one went up to the iwan, with color and scent.
sentences.s699-s14: Five hundred wise and alert servants arrived, great and silver‑adorned.
sentences.s699-s15: From Rûm, likewise, came that attendant of forty fairy‑faced ones, famed and heart‑rending.
sentences.s699-s16: And from those Roman philosophers came four wise, learned, and celebrated men.
sentences.s699-s53: They prepared a selected performance and summoned Niyatous before him.
sentences.s699-s54: Wearing (or with) dishevelled hair and short war-cloaks, together— the leaders of the host, all, great and small.
sentences.s699-s55: He said to them thus: “Now which are the chiefs and which the warlike men?”
sentences.s699-s56: Niyatous chose seventy men to take with him on the day of battle.
sentences.s699-s45: Khosrow's heart laughed because of the renowned army, like a flower at the time of spring.
sentences.s699-s46: His bright, noble heart made him swift; Maran set the burden forward with a heel-stamp.
sentences.s699-s47: He saw Niyatous and took him up (into his arms); he began asking about freedom.
sentences.s699-s48: From the Caesar who endured such hardship, he refused further suffering and emptied his treasury.
sentences.s699-s29: He recorded all this in the inner book; they departed with knowledge and guidance.
sentences.s699-s30: Shamr's star advanced with a guide, to learn when his departure from his place would occur.
sentences.s699-s31: The Caesar stirred on the day of Bahram; by an auspicious star and omen the world shone.
sentences.s699-s32: Caesar was traveling two stages along the road; another came before the army.
sentences.s699-s25: There was a man who, moreover, had a secret: that he would not sell free men for anything.
sentences.s699-s26: No one saw Chokhrad Borzin; if he remained a little while in the world, that would be enough.
sentences.s699-s27: For that reason the God of the world created him, so that what was hidden in him might be made manifest.
sentences.s699-s28: Like the shining sun he is without peer; all his works and deeds are divine.
sentences.s699-s9: From the hangings and the brocade of Rome, with gold-figured designs and from its silk a cloth.
sentences.s699-s10: The same yāre and a collar with earrings, three precious gem‑encrusted crowns.
sentences.s699-s11: He adorned a building, fourfold and golden; its majestic body full of gems of the kingly (treasury).
sentences.s699-s12: Forty other cradles — some of ebony — glittering with jewels like the eye of a rooster.
sentences.s699-s37: Niyātūs was his warlike brother; in that battle he was the commander of his army.
sentences.s699-s38: Maryam said to him, 'By my own blood you are — upon that I will stake it, for you are of the same faith.'
sentences.s699-s39: I entrust to you the maiden as requested; the army is arrayed in this manner.
sentences.s699-s40: Niyātūs accepted outright; it was said, and he turned away weeping.
sentences.s699-s61: He said to those chiefs, 'If the Creator is my ally when the time of battle comes.'
sentences.s699-s62: I will discover my own ability; I will make the earth like the Pleiades.
sentences.s699-s63: Let there be nothing except the thoughts of friends; may heaven be friend and favor, driving harm away from the garden.
sentences.s699-s21: He gave the guests that same horse and dinars; he gave abundantly every appropriate thing.
sentences.s699-s22: Thus he said, 'O subjects of the king: if it is fitting, bring (it) and place it upon the neck like the moon.'
sentences.s699-s23: In all my wanderings I have found no one in the world more deserving among the guests.
sentences.s699-s24: Choshapur was the chief of Karanj; he was prominent in speech at assemblies.
sentences.s699-s1: After that, when he learned the army was coming, the world became surrounded by black horsemen.
sentences.s699-s2: He selected one hundred thousand of those Romans, all famed from the battlefield.
sentences.s699-s3: He demanded arms, dirhams, and war-horses; the foremost of the fighting set out — there was no delay.
sentences.s699-s4: He had a daughter called Maryam, wise and endowed with jewels, counsel, and resolve.
sentences.s699-s5: He sent (her) to the king according to the rites of the faith, wishing a blessing from the Creator.
sentences.s699-s6: He took/accepted his daughter (the گستهم گرد) and, in a fine ceremony, entrusted her to the king.
sentences.s699-s7: And after that he brought so much trousseau that from it were made many fine items.
sentences.s699-s8: From golden things and regal jewels, from rubies and from brocaded garments.
sentences.s699-s33: He ordered that Maryam come forward; she spoke with him beyond measure.
sentences.s699-s34: He said to her, 'Keep your skirt away from the Iranians, and do not loosen the bond between (us).'
sentences.s699-s35: You must not be exposed so that the king may see you, for some new misfortune may befall you.
sentences.s699-s36: He said this and bade her farewell with affection, saying, 'May the heavens be your companion in departure.'
sentences.s699-s17: He told them what ought to be said, and he likewise hid that same thing with Mary.
sentences.s699-s18: From calm and desire and necessity — the same (gave) a gift, a portion, and worthiness.
sentences.s699-s19: Then, at their request, the Roman increased the number beyond three hundred thousand thousands.
sentences.s699-s20: He sent to each person who stood before him a jeweled diadem upon his head.
sentences.s699-s41: The army was moving on the road, Niyātus in front with mace and sword.
sentences.s699-s42: When Khosrow heard that the host had arrived, he led the army out of that town onto the road.
sentences.s699-s43: When the chiefs appeared, the banner-bearers, horsemen and cuirass-clad men came into view.
sentences.s699-s44: The army was going in good order around the horsemen—alert riders and truly heroic men.
